steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage establishes two chemical signatures: red ocher contains iron oxide (high iron), while plant-based pigments contain high carbon. The test found that pigment on several beads had no iron but high carbon. The blank must state what conclusion this chemical fingerprint logically forces — no speculation beyond what the data directly proves.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — the Natufian beadmakers used plant-based pigments rather than ocher to decorate some of the beads examined in the study.: No iron + high carbon = plant-based pigment signature. "Some of the beads" is carefully scoped to the beads that tested positive. This is the direct, necessary conclusion from the chemical evidence given. ✓
  • Choice B — the Natufian beadmakers preferred to use plant-based pigments because they are much brighter than mineral-based pigments are.: The passage never compares the brightness of plant-based vs. mineral-based pigments. Even if plant-based pigments were brighter, the test results cannot tell us why the beadmakers chose them. This introduces unsupported reasoning. ✗
  • Choice C — the pigments used by the Natufian beadmakers likely came from plants because ocher was difficult to find.: The passage says nothing about the availability or scarcity of ocher in the Natufian world. Difficulty of supply is a possible explanation but one the evidence does not support. ✗
  • Choice D — the Natufian beads examined in the study are the oldest surviving examples of the use of plant-based pigments for decorating beads.: The passage does not compare these beads to any other ancient beads or claim primacy. The study only tests these beads — it cannot establish an "oldest" record without a broader comparison the passage never makes. ✗
A is correct.
